title: Nullable value types
instruction:
`int?` is shorthand for `Nullable<int>`. It can be null.

Run this.
successMessage: int? wraps int in a Nullable<int>. HasValue checks if it has a value. Value throws if null — use ?? for safety.

title: Null coalescing ??
instruction:
`??` returns the left side if not null, else the right side.

Run this.
successMessage: ?? is the null-coalescing operator. It is the safe way to provide a fallback without an if-null check.

title: Null conditional ?.
instruction:
`?.` calls a member only if the object is not null, otherwise returns null.

Run this.
successMessage: ?. short-circuits to null if the object is null. Combine with ?? for safe defaults.

title: ??= null coalescing assignment
instruction:
`??=` assigns the right side only if the left is null.

Run this.
successMessage: ??= is shorthand for: if (s == null) s = value. It initialises a variable only when needed.

title: Nullable reference types
instruction:
Enable nullable reference types with `#nullable enable` to get compile-time null safety.

Run this.
successMessage: string? means 'this string can be null'. string means 'never null'. The compiler warns if you dereference a nullable without checking.

title: Null-forgiving operator !
instruction:
The `!` postfix operator tells the compiler 'I know this is not null'.

Use sparingly — it disables the null check.

Run this.
successMessage: ! suppresses the nullable warning. Use it only when you are certain — if you're wrong, you get a NullReferenceException at runtime.

title: Pattern matching for null
instruction:
Use `is null` and `is not null` patterns for readable null checks.

Run this.
successMessage: is null is clearer than == null (avoids operator overloading surprises). Pattern matching unifies null check and type check.

title: Null in collections
instruction:
Collections can contain null values. Use LINQ to filter them.

Run this.
successMessage: Filter nulls with Where(n => n != null). In C# 11 you can use Where(n => n is not null) for the same effect.
